1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
7	
8	/-! # The Jacobian of a smooth proper curve
9	
10	The Jacobian of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field, equipped with
11	its structure as an abelian variety.
12	
13	## Status (Phase C scaffolding)
14	
15	The Jacobian is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness
16	(`JacobianWitness`) for the curve `C`; the four protected instances on `Jacobian C` all
17	project from the witness directly.
18	
19	This file currently contains TWO `sorry`-bodied declarations (the Phase-C scaffolding
20	inventory):
21	
22	1. `genusZeroWitness` (iter-127 scaffold): a `JacobianWitness C` for a genus-`0` curve,
23	   with underlying scheme `Spec k`. The body is a `sorry` to be closed once pieces
24	   (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile land (iter-138+); the
25	   substantive mathematical content is the genus-`0` rigidity statement
26	   `rigidity_over_kbar` (M2.a; `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`).
27	2. `nonempty_jacobianWitness` (Phase-C OFF-LIMITS sorry): existence of an Albanese
28	   witness for an arbitrary smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve; gated on
29	   M2 + M3 closure (Albanese existence in genus `≥ 1` plus genus-`0` rigidity).
30	
31	The file contains:
32	- `IsAlbanese`: the Albanese universal property for a pointed curve.
33	- `IsAlbanese.unique`: uniqueness of the Albanese object up to canonical isomorphism.
34	- `JacobianWitness`: a bundled candidate Albanese object together with the universal
35	  property uniformly over the choice of $k$-rational marked point.
36	- `genusZeroWitness`: an Albanese witness for a genus-`0` curve, body `sorry`
37	  (iter-127 scaffold; closure deferred to iter-138+).
38	- `nonempty_jacobianWitness`: existence of an Albanese witness for every curve —
39	  the headline Phase-C sorry, absorbing both higher-genus Albanese existence and
40	  genus-`0` rigidity.
41	- `Jacobian` and its four protected instances, each obtained by projection from the
42	  Albanese witness.
43	
44	### Forbidden shortcut (sanity check)
45	
46	Defining `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` *unconditionally* (the terminal object,
47	i.e. `Spec k`) compiles and discharges three of the four instances for free, but the
48	fourth instance `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (𝟙_ _).hom` would force `genus C = 0`,
49	which is mathematically wrong for any curve of genus `≥ 1`. The terminal-object definition
50	is therefore forbidden when `genus C > 0`. The witness-based definition avoids this
51	issue: in genus `0` the witness's underlying scheme `J` is `Spec k`, and in genus `> 0`
52	the witness's `J` is the honest Albanese object.
53	-/
54	
55	set_option autoImplicit false
56	
57	universe u
58	
59	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
62	
63	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
64	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
65	  [IsProper C.hom]
66	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
67	
68	/-- The Albanese universal property for a pointed smooth proper curve `(C, P)`.
69	An object `J` (with abelian-variety structure) is the Albanese of `(C, P)` if it
70	receives a universal pointed morphism `C ⟶ J` sending `P` to the identity. -/
71	def IsAlbanese (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
72	    (J : Over (Spec (.of k)))
73	    [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] : Prop :=
74	  ∃ α : C ⟶ J, P ≫ α = η[J] ∧
75	    ∀ {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]
76	      [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (_ : P ≫ f = η[A]),
77	      ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = α ≫ g
78	
79	namespace IsAlbanese
80	
81	noncomputable def ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
82	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
83	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) : C ⟶ J :=
84	  Classical.choose h
85	
86	lemma comp_ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
87	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
88	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) :
89	    P ≫ h.ofCurve = η[J] :=
90	  (Classical.choose_spec h).1
91	
92	lemma exists_unique_ofCurve_comp {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
93	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
94	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J)
95	    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
96	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A]) :
97	    ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = h.ofCurve ≫ g :=
98	  (Classical.choose_spec h).2 f hf
99	
100	/-- The Albanese object is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the
101	universal morphisms. -/
102	theorem unique {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))} {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C}
103	    {J₁ J₂ : Over (Spec (.of k))}
104	    [GrpObj J₁] [IsProper J₁.hom] [Smooth J₁.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₁.hom]
105	    [GrpObj J₂] [IsProper J₂.hom] [Smooth J₂.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₂.hom]
106	    (h₁ : IsAlbanese C P J₁) (h₂ : IsAlbanese C P J₂) :
107	    ∃! (e : J₁ ⟶ J₂), h₂.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ e := by
108	  have ⟨g, hg_eq, hg_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
109	  have ⟨h, hh_eq, hh_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
110	  have ⟨g₁, hg₁_eq, hg₁_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
111	  have g₁_eq_id : g₁ = 𝟙 J₁ := by
112	    refine (hg₁_unique (𝟙 J₁) ?_).symm
113	    simp
114	  have ggh_eq_g₁ : g ≫ h = g₁ := by
115	    refine hg₁_unique (g ≫ h) ?_
116	    change h₁.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ (g ≫ h)
117	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hg_eq, ← hh_eq]
118	  have hgh : g ≫ h = 𝟙 J₁ := by rw [ggh_eq_g₁, g₁_eq_id]
119	  have ⟨k₂, hk₂_eq, hk₂_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
120	  have k₂_eq_id : k₂ = 𝟙 J₂ := by
121	    refine (hk₂_unique (𝟙 J₂) ?_).symm
122	    simp
123	  have hhg_eq_k₂ : h ≫ g = k₂ := by
124	    refine hk₂_unique (h ≫ g) ?_
125	    change h₂.ofCurve = h₂.ofCurve ≫ (h ≫ g)
126	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hh_eq, ← hg_eq]
127	  have hhg : h ≫ g = 𝟙 J₂ := by rw [hhg_eq_k₂, k₂_eq_id]
128	  exact ⟨g, hg_eq, fun g' hg' => hg_unique g' hg'⟩
129	
130	end IsAlbanese
131	
132	/-- The identity morphism on `Spec k` is geometrically irreducible.
133	This is a small helper needed for the genus-0 case of `Jacobian`. -/
134	lemma geometricallyIrreducible_id_Spec (k : Type u) [Field k] :
135	    GeometricallyIrreducible (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))) where
136	  geometrically_irreducibleSpace := by
137	    intro K _ y Z fst snd h
138	    haveI : IsIso snd := h.isIso_snd_of_isIso
139	    exact ObjectProperty.prop_of_iso (P := (IrreducibleSpace · : ObjectProperty Scheme))
140	      (asIso snd).symm inferInstance
141	
142	/-- A bundled Albanese witness for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
143	`C`: a candidate group scheme `J` (with proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible
144	structure, smooth of relative dimension `genus C`), and a proof that `J` satisfies
145	the Albanese universal property of Definition `IsAlbanese` for `C` *for every choice
146	of $k$-rational marked point* `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`.
147	
148	Mathematically, the underlying scheme `J` (and its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
149	structure) is intrinsic to `C`; only the universal morphism `C ⟶ J` depends on the
150	choice of marked point `P`. Encoding `isAlbaneseFor` as a `∀ P, IsAlbanese …` field
151	keeps the bundle independent of any specific pointing, which is what
152	`AbelJacobi.Jacobian.ofCurve P` needs in order to project the Albanese morphism for
153	an *arbitrary* input `P`.
154	
155	The smoothness witness is recorded at the specific relative dimension `genus C`,
156	which is the form required by `Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`. -/
157	structure JacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
158	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] where
159	  /-- The candidate Albanese object. -/
160	  J : Over (Spec (.of k))
161	  /-- The group-scheme structure on `J`. -/
162	  grpObj : GrpObj J
163	  /-- `J` is proper over `k`. -/
164	  proper : IsProper J.hom
165	  /-- `J` is smooth over `k`. -/
166	  smooth : Smooth J.hom
167	  /-- `J` is geometrically irreducible over `k`. -/
168	  geomIrred : GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom
169	  /-- `J` is smooth of relative dimension `genus C` (so dim `J = genus C`). -/
170	  smoothGenus : SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) J.hom
171	  /-- The Albanese universal-property data: for every choice of $k$-rational marked
172	  point `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`, the scheme `J` together with its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
173	  structure satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve `(C, P)`. -/
174	  isAlbaneseFor : ∀ (P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C), @IsAlbanese k _ C P J grpObj proper smooth geomIrred
175	
176	/-- Genus-`0` Albanese witness. The witness object is the trivial terminal scheme
177	`𝟙_ (Over (Spec k))`; the `JacobianWitness` structural fields (proper, smooth,
178	geometrically irreducible, genus-stable, group-object) reduce to the
179	corresponding trivialities on `Spec k` via the structural cluster
180	`(𝟙_).hom = 𝟙 (Spec k)`. The `isAlbaneseFor` field consumes the **genus-0
181	abelian-variety rigidity** statement: for every pointed `(C, P)` and every
182	target group scheme `(A, η[A])`, the genus-0 hypothesis forces a morphism
183	`f : C ⟶ A` with `P ≫ f = η[A]` to equal `toUnit C ≫ η[A]`. Over an
184	algebraically closed base `k̄` this is
185	`AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` (in
186	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`, char-free, route-C, iter-163).
187	For a general `[Field k]` base the keystone is consumed via pullback to
188	`Spec k̄` + descent of the morphism equality along the faithfully-flat
189	surjection `Spec k̄ → Spec k`. The uniqueness clause cancels the epimorphism
190	`toUnit C` via `Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective` (smooth ⟹ flat,
191	geometrically irreducible ⟹ surjective on `Spec k`).
192	
193	**Status (iter-172):** the `key` body remains `sorry`; the rigidity consumer
194	exists (declared) but still propagates upstream sorries from the iter-171
195	body skeleton in `Genus0BaseObjects.lean`. Wiring closes once
196	(i) the `Genus0BaseObjects` body-skeleton internal sorries discharge, and
197	(ii) the `k → k̄` pullback / descent step is filled. -/
198	noncomputable def genusZeroWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
199	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
200	    (h : genus C = 0) :
201	    JacobianWitness C where
202	  J := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))
203	  grpObj := inferInstance
204	  -- `(𝟙_).hom` is defeq `𝟙 (Spec k)`, but instance search does not unfold it;
205	  -- supply each instance for the identity and let term-elaboration close by defeq.
206	  proper := (inferInstance : IsProper (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
207	  smooth := (inferInstance : Smooth (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
208	  geomIrred := geometricallyIrreducible_id_Spec k
209	  smoothGenus := by
210	    rw [h]; exact (inferInstance : SmoothOfRelativeDimension 0 (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
211	  isAlbaneseFor := by
212	    -- For every `k`-rational marked point `P`, exhibit the Albanese datum for
213	    -- the pointed curve `(C, P)` with `J = 𝟙_` (blueprint C.2 / C.3).
214	    intro P
215	    -- The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map `toUnit C`.
216	    refine ⟨toUnit C, ?_, ?_⟩
217	    · -- Pointed condition: both `P ≫ toUnit C` and `η[𝟙_]` are maps `𝟙_ ⟶ 𝟙_`,
218	      -- equal by uniqueness of morphisms into the terminal object.
219	      exact toUnit_unique _ _
220	    · -- Universal factorisation: given `f : C ⟶ A` with `P ≫ f = η[A]`, the
221	      -- factoring morphism `𝟙_ ⟶ A` is the identity element `η[A]`.
222	      intro A _ _ _ _ f _hf
223	      -- Genus-0 RIGIDITY (C.2): `f` equals the constant morphism at the identity.
224	      -- The route-C content `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` (declared in
225	      -- `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`) is char-free and import-clean
226	      -- (proven iter-163 onward). To consume it for the general `[Field k]` goal,
227	      -- this body needs to pull back `(C, A, f, P)` along an algebraic closure
228	      -- `Spec k̄ → Spec k`, apply `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` in the
229	      -- pulled-back setting, then descend the morphism equality along the
230	      -- faithfully-flat surjection (the descent step itself is
231	      -- `Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`, already used below to cancel the
232	      -- terminal epimorphism). The pullback functor + the descent of a morphism
233	      -- equality is the residual sub-build; it is real but is not gated on
234	      -- any out-of-file plan-level decision.
235	      have key : f = toUnit C ≫ η[A] := by
236	        sorry
237	      refine ⟨η[A], key, ?_⟩
238	      -- Uniqueness: any `g'` with `f = toUnit C ≫ g'` equals `η[A]`, by cancelling
239	      -- the epimorphism `toUnit C`. Its underlying scheme morphism is `C.hom`
240	      -- (`Over.toUnit_left`), which is faithfully flat (smooth ⟹ flat) and
241	      -- surjective (a geometrically irreducible curve over a field is nonempty and
242	      -- `Spec k` is a single point), hence an epimorphism (`Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`).
243	      intro g' hg'
244	      have hcancel : toUnit C ≫ g' = toUnit C ≫ η[A] := by rw [← hg', ← key]
245	      have hepiL : Epi (toUnit C).left := by
246	        rw [Over.toUnit_left]; exact Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective C.hom
247	      haveI : Epi (toUnit C) := Over.epi_of_epi_left _
248	      exact (cancel_epi (toUnit C)).mp hcancel
249	
250	/-- The Albanese witness for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C`
251	of positive genus `g ≥ 1` over `k`. The underlying scheme is the Albanese
252	variety of `C` — classically the connected component of the identity of
253	`Pic_{C/k}`, or equivalently the Stein factorisation of the Abel–Jacobi
254	morphism on `Sym^g(C)`. Both constructions require infrastructure not yet
255	in Mathlib (FGA representability of the Picard functor for Route A;
256	symmetric powers + finite-group quotients + Stein factorisation +
257	Brill–Noether–Riemann–Roch for Route B); the iter-123 M3 route audit
258	(`analogies/m3-route-audit.md`) records both at midpoint ~6500 and
259	~9000 LOC respectively.
260	
261	**Status**: iter-134 scaffold — body is `sorry`. The body closure is M3
262	work, currently OFF-CRITICAL-PATH until M2 closes (per STRATEGY.md
263	§ M3, user-escalation-pending).
264	
265	**Iter-135**: the genus-stratified body restructure of
266	`nonempty_jacobianWitness` is now in place (Change 2.1 of iter-135
267	refactor); this scaffold is the positive-genus arm. Body closure
268	remains M3 work (off-critical-path; user-escalation-pending per
269	`analogies/m3-route-audit.md`). -/
270	noncomputable def positiveGenusWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
271	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
272	    (hg : 0 < genus C) :
273	    JacobianWitness C :=
274	  sorry
275	
276	/-- Existence of an Albanese witness for every smooth proper geometrically irreducible
277	curve. This is one of the two open mathematical sorries of the Phase-C Jacobian
278	scaffolding (the other being `genusZeroWitness`); both are scheduled for body
279	closure post M2 + M3 per STRATEGY.md. It packages all five protected sorries
280	(`Jacobian`, `instGrpObj`, `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `instIsProper`,
281	`instGeometricallyIrreducible`) into a single existence hypothesis, *and* records
282	that the Albanese property of the underlying scheme `J` is uniform over every
283	choice of $k$-rational marked point `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C` (via the `isAlbaneseFor`
284	field of `JacobianWitness`).
285	
286	Mathematically this corresponds to the construction of the Albanese variety of `C`,
287	classically via symmetric powers $\mathrm{Sym}^g(C)$ and the Abel--Jacobi map
288	(Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch), or equivalently as the identity component of the
289	Picard scheme. Both routes require infrastructure not yet available in Mathlib
290	(quotients of schemes by finite group actions; FGA representability), so the
291	existence is assumed.
292	
293	**Iter-135 body restructure**: the body is now a `by_cases h :
294	genus C = 0` decomposition consuming `genusZeroWitness` (genus-0 arm)
295	and `positiveGenusWitness` (positive-genus arm). Both arms' bodies
296	remain `sorry` (M2.b body closure iter-138+; M3 body closure
297	off-critical-path); the restructure converts the inline `sorry` here
298	into honest delegation to the two scaffolds, removing one inline-`sorry`
299	site without prejudging either arm's body closure. -/
300	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
301	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
302	    Nonempty (JacobianWitness C) := by
303	  by_cases h : genus C = 0
304	  · exact ⟨genusZeroWitness C h⟩
305	  · exact ⟨positiveGenusWitness C (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero h)⟩
306	
307	/-- A choice of Albanese witness for `C`, extracted via `Classical.choice`.
308	Used to define `Jacobian C` and to discharge each of the four protected instances
309	on it. -/
310	noncomputable def jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
311	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
312	    JacobianWitness C :=
313	  Classical.choice (nonempty_jacobianWitness C)
314	
315	-- data
316	/-- The Jacobian of a smooth, proper curve over a field `k`.
317	
318	The Jacobian is the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness for `C` (see
319	`JacobianWitness` and `nonempty_jacobianWitness`); the existence of such a
320	witness is one of the two open mathematical sorries of the Phase-C scaffolding
321	(the other being `genusZeroWitness`), both scheduled for body closure post
322	M2 + M3 per STRATEGY.md. The genus-`0` specialisation is implicit in the
323	witness — a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over `k` of
324	relative dimension `0` is `Spec k` — and the genus-`0` rigidity content is
325	absorbed into `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. -/
326	noncomputable def Jacobian (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
327	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
328	  (jacobianWitness C).J
329	
330	namespace Jacobian
331	
332	/-! ## The Jacobian of `C` is an abelian variety. -/
333	
334	-- data
335	/-- The group scheme structure on the Jacobian of the curve `C`. -/
336	noncomputable instance instGrpObj : GrpObj (Jacobian C) := (jacobianWitness C).grpObj
337	
338	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is smooth of relative dimension `g` over `k`, where `g` is the
339	genus of `C`. -/
340	instance smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus : SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (Jacobian C).hom :=
341	  (jacobianWitness C).smoothGenus
342	
343	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is proper over `k`. -/
344	instance instIsProper : IsProper (Jacobian C).hom := (jacobianWitness C).proper
345	
346	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is geometrically irreducible over `k`. -/
347	instance instGeometricallyIrreducible : GeometricallyIrreducible (Jacobian C).hom :=
348	  (jacobianWitness C).geomIrred
349	
350	end Jacobian
351	
352	end AlgebraicGeometry

9	/-!
10	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
11	
12	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
13	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
14	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
15	
16	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
17	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
18	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
19	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
20	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
21	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
22	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
23	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
24	relation `D ~ D'`.
25	
26	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
27	
28	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
29	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
30	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`
31	(`RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`), `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), and `RR.4`
32	(`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
33	
34	The 9 pinned declarations are:
35	
36	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
37	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
38	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
39	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
40	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
41	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
42	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).

60	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
61	
62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	For the skeleton we encode a prime divisor by its **generic point** together with
71	a stub witness for the codimension-one + integrality property; this is the
72	standard equivalence (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130: "let `η ∈ Y` be its generic point...
73	the local ring `O_{η,X}` is a discrete valuation ring"). The skeleton's
74	`isCodim1AndIntegral` field is a placeholder `True` that iter-173+ will refine to
75	the genuine `Order.coheight x = 1 ∧ IsIntegral (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint x)`
76	once those notions are extracted; the data field `point` is the substantive one.
77	-/
78	
79	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
80	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
81	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
82	
83	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
84	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
85	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
86	  point : X
87	  /-- Placeholder for `Order.coheight point = 1 ∧ IsIntegral (closure)`; the
88	  codimension-1 + integrality witness. iter-173+ refines this to the real
89	  conjunction once `Order.coheight` is wired through `Scheme.IdealSheafData`. -/
90	  isCodim1AndIntegral : True := trivial
91	
92	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
93	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
94	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
95	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
98	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
99	
100	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
101	
102	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
103	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
104	
105	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
106	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
107	
108	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
111	
112	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
113	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
114	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
115	
116	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
117	
118	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
119	
120	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
121	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
122	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
123	discrete valuation on `f`.
124	
125	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
126	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
127	
128	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
129	
130	iter-173+: the body extracts the DVR `O_{X,η}` (from `(*)` regularity), reads off the
131	`addVal` (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal : R → ℕ∞`), extends it to the fraction field
132	`K(X)`, and produces an `ℤ`-valued valuation on `K(X)^×`. -/
133	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
134	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
135	  sorry
136	
137	end Scheme.RationalMap
138	
139	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
140	
141	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
142	
143	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
144	
145	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
146	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
147	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
148	
149	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
150	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
151	
152	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
153	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
154	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
155	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
156	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
157	
158	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
159	
160	iter-173+: the body promotes the closed point `P` to a `PrimeDivisor` (using the
161	curve-specific closed-point ↔ prime-divisor bijection) and emits
162	`Finsupp.single _ 1`. -/
163	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
164	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
165	  sorry
166	
167	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
168	
169	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
170	
171	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
172	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
173	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
174	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
175	
176	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
177	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
178	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
179	
180	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
181	
182	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
183	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
184	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
185	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
186	
187	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
188	
189	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
190	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
191	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
192	
193	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
194	
195	iter-173+: closes by `Finsupp.sum_add_index` (additivity of the inner monoid
196	hom `fun _ n => n`) + `Finsupp.sum_zero_index` (no support ⇒ empty sum). -/
197	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
198	  sorry
199	
200	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
201	
202	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
203	
204	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
205	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
206	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
207	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
208	
209	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
210	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
211	
212	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
213	following definition, p. 131).
214	
215	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
216	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
217	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
218	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
219	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
220	  sorry
221	
222	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
223	
224	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
225	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
226	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
227	
228	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
229	
230	iter-173+: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`,
231	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
232	union of supports. -/
233	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
234	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
235	  sorry
236	
237	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
238	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
239	
240	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
241	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
242	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
243	
244	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
245	
246	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
247	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
248	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
249	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
250	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
251	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
252	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
253	iters of `RR.1`. -/
254	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
255	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
256	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
257	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
258	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
259	  sorry
260	
261	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
262	
263	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
264	
265	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
266	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
267	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
268	
269	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
270	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
271	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
272	**divisor class group** of `X`.
273	
274	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
275	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
276	
277	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
278	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
279	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
280	
281	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
